title: The case for keeping certain professions cash-only
summary: Argue whether some professions — barbers, food cart vendors, small repair shops — should resist going cashless, and whether refusing cards is a form of dignity rather than backwardness.
topic: Whether certain professions should resist going cashless
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether certain professions — neighbourhood barbers, food cart vendors, small repair shops, market porters — should resist the pressure to install card readers and QR-code payments, and whether keeping cash-only is a defensible choice rather than a sign of backwardness. Anchor in a specific setting (Hanoi, Naples, Tbilisi, Lagos, Bucharest). Use a 'what' cleft, a concession ('Granted,'), at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Take a clear position. Avoid blanket techno-pessimism.
hintStarters: Sitting in the chair of an old barber in central Naples who has refused, for forty years, to take anything but coins, you notice that the conversation he carries on with the customer waiting after you is…
What a cash-only trade actually protects, beyond the obvious convenience of not paying card fees, is something the architects of the cashless transition have rarely cared to…
Granted, the argument for cash-only economies can drift into the kind of romantic anti-modernism that ignores the genuine inconveniences faced by customers who…

hintVocab.meaningVi: sự từ chối có phẩm giá
gửi thu nhập vào ngân hàng

hintVocab.phrase: to settle in cash at the door
hintVocab.meaningVi: thanh toán bằng tiền mặt tại cửa

sampleText:
Sitting in the chair of an old barber in central Naples who has refused, for forty years, to take anything but coins, you notice that the conversation he carries on with the customer waiting after you is genuinely informal in a way that no card-reading transaction would permit. He asks after the man's wife. He remembers, without prompting, that the man's cousin was unwell. When the haircut ends, money is counted into his hand without ceremony and the next customer takes the chair. The whole interaction lasts ninety seconds at the till. Nothing is logged.

What a cash-only trade actually protects, beyond the obvious convenience of not paying card fees, is something the architects of the cashless transition have rarely cared to investigate honestly. They believe they are eliminating friction. They are also eliminating a particular form of small-scale autonomy: the right of a barber or a porter or a market stallholder to set their own prices, accept their own customers, and keep their own pace, without their working day being instrumented by a payment processor in another country.

Granted, the argument for cash-only economies can drift into the kind of romantic anti-modernism that ignores the genuine inconveniences faced by customers who no longer carry cash, particularly tourists and younger urban residents. A barber who refuses cards can lose ten per cent of his potential walk-ins, and refusing the change is not a costless statement of principle. Cash-only operation also masks income from tax authorities in ways that have legitimately worried governments, and the moralising defence of small traders should not be allowed to disguise this fact.

Even so, the wholesale push to make every transaction cashless is not the neutral modernisation it is sold as. Once a barber takes cards, his income becomes visible to processors, to the tax office, and, in some regulatory regimes, to credit-scoring algorithms he has never heard of. The pricing he was able to set informally — five euros for a pensioner he has known since the 1990s, ten for a stranger — becomes harder to defend in a system where every transaction is logged at the listed rate. A small flexibility that was the substance of a forty-year relationship is removed in the name of frictionlessness.

The defensible position is that the choice of payment method should be one the small trader keeps, not one the city or the platform takes from them. A barber, a porter, or a fruit-stall owner who wants to operate cash-only should be allowed to do so without being publicly framed as backward, and a barber who wants to take cards should be supported in doing so without being charged ruinous fees. What is lost when this choice is removed is not nostalgia. It is the autonomy that made the small trade worth doing in the first place.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to expose what the cashless transition actually erases
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a cash-only trade actually protects, beyond the obvious convenience of not paying card fees, is something the architects of the cashless transition have rarely cared to investigate honestly' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' với một cụm chèn ('beyond the obvious convenience…') để loại bỏ câu trả lời hiển nhiên trước khi đưa ra câu trả lời thực sự của bài luận. Đây là cách tinh tế để báo hiệu cho người đọc rằng bài viết sẽ vượt qua sự thật bề nổi.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a cash-only trade actually protects, beyond the obvious convenience of not paying card fees, is something the architects of the cashless transition have rarely cared to investigate honestly' uses a what-cleft with an inserted phrase ('beyond the obvious convenience…') to rule out the surface answer before delivering the essay's real one. It is a subtle way of telling the reader that the writing will go past the obvious truth.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive 'is removed in the name of' to background the agent
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'A small flexibility that was the substance of a forty-year relationship is removed in the name of frictionlessness' đặt 'sự linh hoạt' vào vị trí chủ ngữ và che giấu chủ thể loại bỏ nó. Cụm 'in the name of' báo hiệu một cái cớ chính thức được sử dụng để biện minh cho hành động — đây là cách dùng giọng bị động kết hợp với một cụm giới từ để chỉ ra ai đó đang biện minh mà không cần nêu tên họ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'A small flexibility that was the substance of a forty-year relationship is removed in the name of frictionlessness' places 'flexibility' in subject position and hides who is removing it. 'In the name of' flags an official excuse being used to justify the action — a B2 use of voice combined with a prepositional phrase to indict an institution without naming it.

sampleTranslationVi:
Ngồi trên ghế của một thợ cắt tóc già ở trung tâm Naples, người đã từ chối, trong bốn mươi năm, nhận bất cứ thứ gì ngoài tiền xu, bạn nhận ra rằng cuộc trò chuyện ông tiếp tục với người khách đang chờ sau bạn thực sự là không chính thức theo cách mà không giao dịch đọc thẻ nào cho phép. Ông hỏi thăm vợ của người đàn ông. Ông nhớ, mà không cần được nhắc, rằng người em họ của ông ta đã ốm. Khi cắt tóc xong, tiền được đếm vào tay ông không có nghi thức gì và khách tiếp theo lên ghế. Toàn bộ tương tác kéo dài chín mươi giây ở quầy thu ngân. Không có gì được ghi lại.

Điều mà một ngành nghề chỉ nhận tiền mặt thực sự bảo vệ, vượt ra ngoài sự tiện lợi hiển nhiên của việc không phải trả phí thẻ, là điều mà các kiến trúc sư của quá trình chuyển đổi không tiền mặt hiếm khi quan tâm điều tra một cách trung thực. Họ tin rằng họ đang loại bỏ ma sát. Họ cũng đang loại bỏ một hình thức tự chủ quy mô nhỏ cụ thể: quyền của một thợ cắt tóc hay một người khuân vác hay một chủ quầy chợ tự đặt giá, tự chấp nhận khách, và giữ nhịp độ của mình, mà không cần ngày làm việc bị đo đạc bởi một bộ xử lý thanh toán ở một nước khác.

Công nhận, lập luận cho các nền kinh tế chỉ tiền mặt có thể trôi dạt vào loại chủ nghĩa chống hiện đại lãng mạn bỏ qua những sự bất tiện thực sự mà khách hàng không còn mang tiền mặt phải đối mặt, đặc biệt là khách du lịch và cư dân đô thị trẻ. Một thợ cắt tóc từ chối thẻ có thể mất mười phần trăm số khách tiềm năng đi vào, và từ chối thay đổi không phải là một tuyên bố nguyên tắc không tốn kém. Hoạt động chỉ tiền mặt cũng che giấu thu nhập khỏi cơ quan thuế theo những cách đã chính đáng lo lắng các chính phủ, và việc bênh vực mang tính đạo đức cho các tiểu thương không nên được phép che giấu sự thật này.

Dẫu vậy, việc thúc đẩy toàn bộ làm mọi giao dịch không tiền mặt không phải là sự hiện đại hóa trung lập như nó được bán ra. Một khi thợ cắt tóc nhận thẻ, thu nhập của ông trở nên có thể nhìn thấy đối với các bộ xử lý, đối với cơ quan thuế, và, trong một số chế độ pháp lý, đối với các thuật toán chấm điểm tín dụng mà ông chưa bao giờ nghe đến. Việc định giá mà ông có thể đặt một cách không chính thức — năm euro cho một người về hưu mà ông biết từ những năm 1990, mười cho một người lạ — trở nên khó bảo vệ hơn trong một hệ thống nơi mọi giao dịch được ghi lại ở mức giá niêm yết. Một sự linh hoạt nhỏ vốn là bản chất của một mối quan hệ bốn mươi năm bị loại bỏ nhân danh sự không có ma sát.

Lập trường có thể bảo vệ được là lựa chọn phương thức thanh toán nên là cái mà tiểu thương giữ, không phải cái mà thành phố hay nền tảng lấy đi từ họ. Một thợ cắt tóc, một người khuân vác, hay một chủ quầy trái cây muốn chỉ nhận tiền mặt nên được phép làm như vậy mà không bị đóng khung công khai là lạc hậu, và một thợ cắt tóc muốn nhận thẻ nên được hỗ trợ làm như vậy mà không bị tính phí hủy diệt. Cái bị mất khi lựa chọn này bị loại bỏ không phải là nỗi nhớ. Đó là sự tự chủ đã làm cho ngành nghề nhỏ đáng làm ngay từ đầu.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to expose what cash-only trade really protects?
options: What a cash-only trade actually protects, beyond the obvious convenience of not paying card fees, is something the architects of the cashless transition have rarely cared to investigate honestly.
Card fees a cash-only trade actually protects beyond the obvious convenience the architects rarely cared what is.
Honestly investigate rarely cared to have what a cash-only trade actually protects the architects beyond the obvious convenience.
The cashless transition is something what protects beyond the obvious convenience of not paying card fees a cash-only trade actually.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … is …' with an inserted phrase ('beyond the obvious convenience…') rules out the surface answer before delivering the essay's real one.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … beyond the obvious …, is …'.

options: A bank that has been built underground
Lacking adequate access to mainstream banking services — accounts, cards, credit — despite being economically active
Owing money to too many banks
Having too many bank accounts
mcQuestions.explanation: Underbanked people earn and spend money but do not have full access to bank services — they often depend on cash-based work to function economically.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'under' do to 'banked' as a status?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'Granted,' as a concession opener?
options: Granted, the argument for cash-only economies can drift into the kind of romantic anti-modernism that ignores genuine inconveniences.
The argument granted for cash-only economies can drift into the kind of romantic anti-modernism that ignores genuine inconveniences.
Drift into the kind of romantic anti-modernism granted the argument for cash-only economies that ignores genuine inconveniences can.
Ignores genuine inconveniences the argument can drift granted for cash-only economies into the kind of romantic anti-modernism.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted,' followed by a complete clause is the cleanest B2 concession opener.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Granted,' before a full sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction backgrounds who is removing the barber's flexibility?
options: A small flexibility that was the substance of a forty-year relationship is removed in the name of frictionlessness.
Removed in the name of frictionlessness a small flexibility the substance of a forty-year relationship is that was.
Frictionlessness in the name of a small flexibility removed is that was the substance of a forty-year relationship.
Was the substance of a forty-year relationship is removed in the name of frictionlessness a small flexibility that.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'is removed in the name of frictionlessness' is a passive that hides the agent (banks, platforms, regulators) while naming the official excuse.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses 'is removed in the name of…'?

options: Payment made with very smooth coins
A payment so fast and easy that it disappears as a moment in the transaction — usually achieved by removing the manual handling of cash
Payment made without any disputes between parties
Payment that requires no money at all
mcQuestions.explanation: Frictionless payment is the industry term for transactions designed to vanish — and the essay argues that what disappears with the friction is also a form of human relationship.
mcQuestions.hint: What is 'friction' in payments, and what does 'less' do?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence opens with a participle clause to set the analytical scene?
options: Sitting in the chair of an old barber in central Naples who has refused, for forty years, to take anything but coins, you notice that the conversation he carries on with the customer waiting after you is genuinely informal.

You notice sitting in the chair of an old barber in central Naples who has refused for forty years to take anything but coins that the conversation he carries on with the customer waiting after you is genuinely informal.

Genuinely informal the conversation he carries on with the customer waiting after you is sitting in the chair of an old barber in central Naples you notice who has refused for forty years to take anything but coins.

For forty years to take anything but coins who has refused sitting in the chair of an old barber in central Naples you notice that the conversation he carries on with the customer waiting after you is genuinely informal.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Sitting in the chair of an old barber in central Naples…' is a present-participle clause opener that sets the analytical scene before the main clause delivers the observation.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for an -ing phrase at the start of the sentence, separated by a comma from the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example, all banks are evil.
Even so, the wholesale push to make every transaction cashless is not the neutral modernisation it is sold as.
On the contrary, no trader has ever taken a card.
Furthermore, all cash is gold.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' grants the previous concession (tax issues are real) and pivots back to the writer's main argument (the cashless push is not as neutral as it claims).
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the concession and reasserts the writer's broader position?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'instrumented' mean in the phrase 'their working day being instrumented by a payment processor'?
options: Played on a musical instrument
Measured, logged, and made legible to outside systems — every transaction recorded as data
Fitted with surgical instruments
Coordinated by a conductor
mcQuestions.explanation: To instrument a process is to measure it. When a working day is instrumented, every transaction becomes data — visible to processors, tax authorities, and credit-scoring systems.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'instrumented' do in engineering — how does it relate to measurement?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Honestly, going cashless is just basically killing small businesses, no question.
It's kind of obvious that barbers should just be allowed to take cash, full stop.
The compulsory adoption of card-based payment systems in small-scale trade represents not a neutral modernisation but a redistribution of operational autonomy from the trader to the processor.
Cash is like, you know, supposed to be how barbers work and stuff.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses formal vocabulary ('compulsory adoption', 'redistribution of operational autonomy') and avoids contractions, slang, and informal markers.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: All trades must remain cash-only forever.
All trades must go cashless immediately.

The choice of payment method should remain with the small trader; both cash-only and card-taking should be supported, and the loss of that choice — not the choice itself — is what damages small trade.
Small traders should pay their workers in card transactions only.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay defends autonomy of choice rather than cash itself — the lost good is the trader's right to decide.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph five for the writer's specific verdict.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'five euros for a pensioner he has known since the 1990s, ten for a stranger'?
options: Fixed pricing that all customers pay equally
Informal, relationship-based pricing in which long-standing customers receive a different price than newcomers — a flexibility hard to maintain when every transaction is logged at the listed rate
A bulk discount available to all senior citizens
Random pricing with no logic
mcQuestions.explanation: The example illustrates the kind of informal pricing flexibility cash permits — and that visible, logged transactions quietly erase.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the social logic behind charging old customers differently?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to keep the books simple' mean as the essay uses it?
options: To use shorter accounting books
To maintain a minimal, manageable record of transactions appropriate to the scale of the business — not the audit-ready detail that card systems generate automatically
To stop reading complex literature
To write only simple words in the books
mcQuestions.explanation: Small traders often need simple records; mandatory card systems impose more detailed records than the trader's actual operation requires.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'books' mean in accounting, and what would 'simple' add to that?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a defining relative clause correctly?
options: A barber who refuses cards can lose ten per cent of his potential walk-ins.
Refuses cards a barber who can lose ten per cent of his potential walk-ins.
Of his potential walk-ins can lose a barber who refuses cards ten per cent.
Potential walk-ins ten per cent of his a barber who refuses cards can lose.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'who refuses cards' is a defining relative clause specifying exactly which barber the writer means.
mcQuestions.hint: Defining relative clauses follow the noun directly and start with 'who', 'that', or 'which'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to skim off the top' mean?
options: To remove cream from milk
To take a small percentage from a transaction stream — typically through fees — that adds up to a large extraction over time
To swim across the surface of a pool
To remove dust from a desk
mcQuestions.explanation: Payment processors skim off the top through transaction fees — small per-transaction percentages that quietly extract significant value from small trades.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'skim' mean, and what is 'the top' of a transaction?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence closes the essay with a sharp, summary verdict?
options: In conclusion, payment methods are complicated.
Therefore, all card payment must be banned by law immediately.
What is lost when this choice is removed is not nostalgia. It is the autonomy that made the small trade worth doing in the first place.
To sum up, both cash and card have advantages.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C closes with a sharp redefinition: what cashless removal takes is not sentimental attachment but trader autonomy.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option ends with a redefinition of what is actually being lost?

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what becomes harder for a barber once he accepts cards?
options: Cutting hair to the customer's satisfaction
Setting informal, relationship-based prices — different prices for different customers — because every transaction is now logged at the listed rate
Talking to customers about their families
Finding new customers
mcQuestions.explanation: Paragraph four names this exact loss: the informal flexibility of pricing the barber for a long-standing pensioner becomes hard to defend in a logged-transaction system.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph four for the writer's specific example of what informal pricing loses.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a small trader in your neighbourhood — a barber, a food vendor, a tailor — who still works mostly in cash. What would they actually lose if they were required to install a card reader tomorrow? Would you, as a customer, miss anything? What policy would you propose to protect their choice without making tax evasion easier?
